titleStmt.title: SII 1.151: original edition by Eugen Hultzsch
PART IV. ADDENDA. No. 151. A PALLAVA GRANT FROM K≈™RAM.

summary.p: The original of the subjoined grant was bought for Government from the DharmakartƒÅ of K≈´ram, a village near KƒÅ√±chƒ´puram.Sewell's Lists of Antiquities, Vol. I, p. 187. It is engraved on seven thin copper-plates, each of which measures 10(1/8) by 3(1/4) inches. As the plates are in very bad preservation, the work of deciphering them was somewhat difficult. Of the seventh plate about one half is completely lost. Next to it, the first, fifth and sixth plates have suffered most. An elliptic ring, which is about (3/8) inch thick and measures 4 by 4(3/4) inches in diameter, is passed through a hole on the left side of each plate. The seal is about 2(1/2) inches in diameter and bears a bull, which is seated on a pedestal, faces the left and is surmounted by the moon and a li·πÖga. Farther up, there are a few much obliterated syllables. A legend of many letters passes round the whole seal. Unfortunately it is so much worn, that I have failed to decipher it.

The language of the first 4(1/2) plates of the inscription is Sanskrit,‚Äîverse and prose; the remainder is written in Tamil. The Sanskrit portion opens with three benedictory verses, of which the two first are addressed to ≈öiva and the third mentions the race of the Pallavas. Then follows, as usual,Compare the pedigrees on pages 9 and 25, above, and Ind. Ant. Vol. VIII, p. 277. a mythical genealogy of Pallava, the supposed founder of the Pallava race:‚Äî
[[genealogical table:]] Brahman. A·πÖgiras. B·πõihaspati. BharadvƒÅja. Dro·πáa. A≈õvatthƒÅman. Pallava.

The historical part of the inscription describes three kings, viz., Parame≈õvaravarman, his father Mahendravarman and his grandfather Narasi·πÅhavarman. Of Narasi·πÅhavarman it says, that he ‚Äúrepeatedly defeated the Cho·∏∑as, Kera·∏∑as, Ka·∏∑abhras and PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas,‚Äù that he ‚Äúwrote the (three) syllables of (the word) vijaya (i.e., victory), as on a plate, on Pulake≈õin's back, which was caused to be visible (i.e., whom he caused to turn his back) in the battles of Pariya·∏∑a, Ma·πáima·πÖgala, ≈ö≈´ramƒÅra, etc.,‚Äù and that he ‚Äúdestroyed (the city of) VƒÅtƒÅpi.‚Äù No historical information is given about Mahendravarman, who, accordingly, seems to have been an insignificant ruler. A laudatory description of the virtues and deeds of his son Parame≈õvaravarman fills two plates of the inscription. The only historical fact contained in this long and difficult passage is that, in a terrible battle, he ‚Äúmade VikramƒÅditya,‚Äîwhose army consisted of several lakshas,‚Äîtake to flight, covered only by a rag.‚Äù

The three kings who are mentioned in the K≈´ram grant, viz., Narasi·πÅhavarman, Mahendravarman and Parame≈õvaravarman, are identical with three Pallava kings described in Mr. Foulkes' grant of Nandivarman Pallavamalla,Ind. Ant. Vol. VIII, p. 275; Salem Manual, Vol. II, p. 356. The following corrections have to be made in the transcript:‚ÄîLine 13, read Si·πÅhavish·πáor api; line 14, Pariya·∏∑a-Ma·πái¬∫; line 16, Peruva·∏∑anall≈´[r]yyuddhe. viz., Narasi·πÅhavarman I., Mahendravarman II. and Parame≈õvaravarman I. Of Narasi·πÅhavarman I. the lastmentioned grant likewise states, that he ‚Äúdestroyed VƒÅtƒÅpi‚Äù and that he ‚Äúfrequently defeated VallabharƒÅja at Pariya·∏∑a, Ma·πáima·πÖgala, ≈ö≈´ramƒÅra, and other (places).‚Äù Here VallabharƒÅja corresponds to the Pulake≈õin of the K≈´ram grant. If Mr. Foulkes' grant further reports, that Parame≈õvaravarman I. ‚Äúdefeated the army of Vallabha in the battle of Peruva·∏∑anall≈´r,‚Äù it is evident that it alludes to the same fight as is described in the K≈´ram grant.

If we combine the historical information contained in both grants, it appears‚Äî1. that the Pallava king Narasi·πÅhavarman I. defeated Pulake≈õin, alias VallabharƒÅja, at Pariya·∏∑a, Ma·πáima·πÖgala, ≈ö≈´ramƒÅra, and other places, and destroyed VƒÅtƒÅpi, the capital of the Western Chalukyas, and‚Äî2. that his grandson Parame≈õvaravarman I. defeated VikramƒÅditya, alias Vallabha, at Peruva·∏∑anall≈´r. As stated above (p. 11), Pulake≈õin and VikramƒÅditya, the opponents of the two Pallava kings, must have been the Western Chalukya kings Pulike≈õin II. (≈öaka 532 and 556) and his son VikramƒÅditya I. (≈öaka 592 (?) to 602 (?)), who, more indico, likewise boast of having conquered their antagonists.Similarly, RƒÅjendra-Cho·∏∑a and Jayasi·πÅha III. claim to have conquered each other (see page 96, above). And, in a MahobƒÅ inscription (Journal of the German Oriental Society, Vol. XL, p. 50) and in the prologue of the drama Prabodhachandrodaya the Chandella king Kƒ´rtivarman and his general GopƒÅla are said to have completely defeated Kar·πáa of Chedi or ·∏åƒÅhƒÅla, who, in the VikramƒÅ·πÖkacharita (sarga xviii, verse 93), is called ‚Äúthe death to the lord of the KƒÅla√±jara mountain‚Äù (i.e., to the Chandella king). Thus, a grant of Pulike≈õin II. says, that ‚Äúhe caused the leader of the Pallavas to hide his prowess behind the ramparts of KƒÅ√±chƒ´pura;‚ÄùInd. Ant. Vol. VIII, p. 245. and, in a grant of VikramƒÅditya I., it is said that ‚Äúthis lord of the earth, conquering ƒ™≈õvarapotarƒÅja (i.e., Parame≈õvaravarman I.), took KƒÅ√±chƒ´, whose huge walls were insurmountable and hard to be broken, which was surrounded by a large moat that was unfathomable and hard to be crossed, and which resembled the girdle (kƒÅ√±chƒ´) of the southern region (read dakshi·πáadi≈õa·∏•).‚ÄùInd. Ant. Vol. VI, p. 77. The corrupt passage, which precedes the sentence quoted in the text, mentions a ‚Äú≈örƒ´vallabha, who had crushed the fame of Narasi·πÅha, destroyed the power of Mahendra and surpassed ƒ™≈õvara by his polity.‚Äù In whatever way the next following lines have to emended, there is, I think, little doubt, that ≈örƒ´vallabha must refer to either Pulike≈õin II. or VikramƒÅditya I., and Narasi·πÅha, Mahendra and ƒ™≈õvara to the three consecutive Pallava kings Narasi·πÅhavarman I., Mahendravarman II. and Parame≈õvaravarman I.

Another Pallava king, viz., Nandipotavarman, is mentioned as the opponent of the Western Chalukya king VikramƒÅditya II. (≈öaka 655 to 669) in the Vakkaleri grant, which was published by Mr. Rice.Ind. Ant. Vol. VIII, pp. 23 ff. The table inserted on p. 11, above, shows that this Nandipotavarman must be identical with the Pallava king Nandivarman Pallavamalla, who is mentioned in Mr. Foulkes' grant. Though digressing from my subject, I now subjoin a transcript from the facsimile and a translation of that part of the Vakkaleri grant, which describes the reign of VikramƒÅditya II.

p.foreign:
sakalabhuvanasƒÅmrƒÅjyalak·π£mƒ´svaya·πÅvarƒÅbhi·π£ekasamayƒÅnanta
rasamupajƒÅtamahotsƒÅha·∏• ƒÄtmava·πÅ≈õajap≈´rvvan·πõpaticchƒÅyƒÅ
pahƒÅri·πáa·∏• prak·πõtyamitrasya pallavasya sam≈´lonm≈´la
nƒÅya k·πõtamatiratitvarayƒÅ tu·πÅ·∏çƒÅkavi·π£aya·πÅ prƒÅpyƒÅbhimuskhƒÅgatannandipotava
rmmƒÅbhidhƒÅnampallava·πÅ ra·πáamukhe sa·πÅprah·πõtya prapalƒÅsyya ka·π≠umukhavƒÅdi
trasamudragho·π£ƒÅbhidhƒÅnavƒÅdyavi≈õe·π£ƒÅnau kha·π≠vƒÅ·πÅ·πÖgadhvaja·πÅ prabh≈´taprakhyƒÅta
hastivarƒÅnsvakira·πáanikaravikƒÅsanirƒÅk·πõtatimirammƒÅ·πáikyarƒÅ≈õi
√±ca hastek·πõtya kala≈õabhavanilayaharida·πÅganƒÅ·πÅcitakƒÅ·πÅcƒ´ya
mƒÅnƒÅ·πÅ kƒÅ·πÅcƒ´mavinƒÅ≈õya pravi≈õya satataprav·πõttadƒÅnƒÅnƒÅanditadvijja
dƒ´nƒÅnƒÅthajano narasi·πÅhapotavarmmanirmmƒÅpita≈õilƒÅmayarƒÅja
si·πÅhe≈õvarƒÅdidevakulasuvar·πáarƒÅ≈õipratyarppa·πáopƒÅrjitorjitapu·πáya·∏• A
nivƒÅritapratƒÅpaprasarapratƒÅpitapƒÅ·πá·∏çyaco·∏∑akera·∏∑aka·∏∑abhrapra
bh·πõtirƒÅjanyaka·∏• k·π£ubhitakarimakarakarahatadalita≈õuktimuktamuktƒÅphala
prakaramarƒ´cijƒÅlavilasitavelƒÅku≈´le gh≈´r·πáamƒÅnƒÅr·πáobhidhƒÅne dak·π£i·πáƒÅ
r·πáave ≈õaradamala≈õa≈õadharavi≈õadaya≈õorƒÅ≈õimaya·πÅ jayastambha
mati·π£·π≠hipadvikramƒÅdityasatyƒÅ≈õraya≈õrƒ´p·πõthivƒ´vallabhamahƒÅrƒÅjƒÅdhirƒÅ
japarame≈õvarabha·π≠·π≠ƒÅraka·∏•

summary.p: ‚ÄúVikramƒÅditya SatyƒÅ≈õraya ≈örƒ´-P·πõithivƒ´-vallabha, the king of great kings, the supreme ruler, the lord,‚Äîto whom arose great energy immediately after the time of his anointment at the self-choice of the goddess of the sovereignty of the whole world, and who resolved to uproot completely his natural enemy, the Pallava, who had robbed of their splendour the previous kings born from his race,‚Äîreached with great speed the Tu·πá·∏çƒÅkavishaya (i.e., the To·πá·∏çai-ma·πá·∏çalamSee page 106, note 3.), attacked at the head of a battle and put to flight the Pallava, called Nandipotavarman, who had come to meet him, took possession of the musical instrument (called) ‚Äúharsh-sounding‚Äù and of the excellent musical instrument called ‚Äúroar of the sea,‚Äù of the banner (marked with ≈öiva's) club, of many renowned and excellent elephants, and of a heap of rubies, which drove away darkness by the light of the multitude of their rays, and entered (the city of) KƒÅ√±chƒ´,‚Äîwhich seemed to be the handsome girdle (kƒÅ√±chƒ´) of the nymph of the southern region,‚Äîwithout destroying it. Having made the twice-born, the distressed and the helpless rejoice by continual gifts, having acquired great merit by granting heaps of gold to (the temple) of stone (called) RƒÅjasi·πÅhe≈õvara, which Narasi·πÅhapotavarman had caused to be built,See page 11, above. and to other temples, and having burnt by the unimpeded progress of his power the PƒÅ·πá·∏çya, Cho·∏∑a, Kera·∏∑a, Ka·∏∑abhra and other princes, he placed a pillar of victory (jayastambha), which consisted (as it were) of the mass of his fame that was as pure as the bright moon in autumn, on the Southern Ocean, which was called Gh≈´r·πáamƒÅnƒÅr·πáas (i.e., that whose waves are rolling) and whose shore glittered with the rays of the pearls, which had dropped from the shells, that were beaten and split by the trunks of the frightened elephants (of his enemies), which resembled sea-monsters.‚Äù

That VikramƒÅditya II. really entered KƒÅ√±chƒ´ and visited the RƒÅjasi·πÅhe≈õvara Temple, is proved by a much obliterated Kanarese inscription in the KailƒÅsanƒÅtha Temple at KƒÅ√±chƒ´puram. This inscription is engraved on the back of a pillar in the ma·πá·∏çapa in front of the RƒÅjasi·πÅhe≈õvara Shrine, close to the east wall of that ma·πá·∏çapa, which at a later time was erected between the front ma·πá·∏çapa and RƒÅjasi·πÅhe≈õvara. It begins with the name of ‚ÄúVikramƒÅditya SatyƒÅ≈õraya ≈örƒ´-P·πõithivƒ´-vallabha, the king of great kings, the supreme ruler, the lord‚Äù and mentions the temple of RƒÅjasi·πÅhe≈õvara (rƒÅjasi·πÅghe≈õvara, line 4).

I now return to the K≈´ram plates. The three last of them contain the grant proper, and record in Sanskrit and Tamil, that Parame≈õvara (i.e., Parame≈õvaravarman I.) gave away the village of Parame≈õvara-ma·πÖgalam,‚Äîwhich was evidently named after the king himself,‚Äîin twenty-five parts. Of these, three were enjoyed by two BrƒÅhma·πáas, Ananta≈õivƒÅchƒÅrya and Phulla≈õarman, who performed the divine rites and looked after the repairs of the ≈öiva temple at K≈´ram, which was called VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta-Pallava-Parame≈õvara, and which had been built by VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta-Pallava, probably a relative of the king. The fourth part was set aside for the cost of providing water and fire for the ma·πá·∏çapa at K≈´ram, and the fifth for reciting the BhƒÅrata in this ma·πá·∏çapa. The remaining twenty parts were given to twenty Chaturvedins.

At the time of the grant, the village of K≈´ram belonged to the nƒÅ·∏çu (country) or, in Sanskrit, manyavƒÅntara-rƒÅsh·π≠ra of Nƒ´rve·∏∑≈´r, a division of ≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukko·π≠·π≠am (lines 49 and 57 f.), and the village of Parame≈õvarama·πÖgalam belonged to the Pa·πâmƒÅ-nƒÅ·∏çu or PatmƒÅ-manyavƒÅntara-rƒÅsh·π≠ra, a division of Ma·πáayi·πüko·π≠·π≠am (lines 53 and 71). As, in numerous Tamil inscriptions, pa·πâma corresponds to the Sanskrit varman,‚Äîthe form Pa·πâmƒÅnƒÅ·∏çu, which occurs also in No. 86, might mean the country of the VarmƒÅs, i.e., of the Pallavas, whose names end in varman, the nominative case of which is varmƒÅ. There is, however, a possibility of patmƒÅ being a mistake for, and pa·πâmƒÅ a Tamil form of, PadmƒÅ,The usual Tamil form of padmƒÅ is patumai. But compare ƒÅttumƒÅ and ƒÅ·πâmƒÅ, both of which correspond to the Sanskrit ƒÅtmƒÅ. one of the names of the goddess Lakshmƒ´. With Ma·πáayi·πüko·π≠·π≠am compare Ma·πáavi·πüko·π≠·π≠am in No. 86 and Eyi·πüko·π≠·π≠am in No. 88. Possibly Ma·πáavi·πüko·π≠·π≠am is a mere corruption of Ma·πáayi·πüko·π≠·π≠am, and Ma·πáayil stands for Ma·πá-eyil, ‚Äúmud-fort,‚Äù which might be a fuller form of Eyil, a village in the South Arcot District, which seems to have given its name to Eyi·πüko·π≠·π≠am.See page 123, note 5.

In conclusion, an important palaeographical peculiarity of the Tamil portion of the K≈´ram plates has to be noted. The pu·∏∑·∏∑i, which corresponds to the NƒÅgarƒ´ virƒÅma, occurs frequently, though not regularly, in combination with seven letters of the Tamil alphabet. In the case of five of these (·πÖ, m, l, ·∏∑, ·πâ) it is represented by a short vertical stroke over the letter, as in the inscription No. 82, above.See page 113, note 6. In the case of the two others (n and r) it has a similar shape, but is placed behind the letter and at an angle with it, in such a way that the lower part is nearer to the letter than the upper one.

revisionDesc.change: Further conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)
Conversion of digital encoding to DHARMA encoding scheme according to EGD (Encoding Guide for Diplomatic Editions)

ti ya·∏• ≈õakti·πÅ kriyƒÅsƒÅdhanƒ´·πÅ trayyƒÅtmƒÅ parame≈õvarassa jayati trailokyac≈´·∏∑ƒÅma·πái·∏• . 2 A

n·πõtavyƒÅhƒÅrajihmƒÅnanam¬∑ . jƒÅta·πÅ yatra nare≈õvaranna ≈õru·πõ·πáumo yuddhe·π£u vƒÅ viklavam¬∑
nirvvighna·∏•·πÅ p·πõthivƒ´nnirƒ´timavatƒÅntatpallavƒÅnƒÅ·πÅ kulam¬∑ . 3 brahma·πáo·πÅgirƒÅstato b·πõha
spati·∏• tasmƒÅdbharadvƒÅja·∏• tato dro·πáa·∏• dro·πáƒÅdaparimitatejodhƒÅmƒÅ≈õvatthƒÅmƒÅ
tato nirƒÅk·πõtakulƒÅpallava·∏• pallava·∏• yatassakalabhuvanavallabha·∏•pallavakulam¬∑ .

dudayadhara·πáidharasomƒÅdityasyƒÅvinatamukha·∏•n·πõpatim≈´rddhni cajo≈õane pratityantrthirƒÅjagaja

sya muhuravajitaco·∏∑akera·∏∑aka·∏∑abhrapƒÅ·πá·∏çyasya sahasrabƒÅhoriva samara≈õatani
rvvi·π£·π≠asahasrajbƒÅhukarmma·πáa·∏• pariya·∏∑ama·πáima·πÅgala≈õ≈´ramƒÅraprabh·πõtira·πáavida≈õ≈õr≈õitapula
ke≈õi·π£·π≠a·π£·π≠hapa·π≠·π≠alikhitavijayƒÅk·π£arasya kala≈õayoneriva vimathitavƒÅtƒÅpe·∏• pautro ma
hendrasyeva suracitasampado mahendravarmma·πáa·∏• supra·πáƒ´tavar·πá·πáƒÅ≈õramadharmmasya putra·∏•
parame≈õvara Iva sarvvƒÅdhikadar≈õana·∏• parame≈õvaravarmmƒÅ bharata Iva sarvvadamana·∏• sagara Iva k·πõtƒÅ
sama√±jasatyƒÅga·∏• kar·πá·πáa Iva pu·π£kalƒÅ·πÅgo ya·∏• priyakaƒÅvyo yayƒÅtiriva AnupanatƒÅnƒÅ·πÅ rƒÅj√±ƒÅ·πÅ
yasyƒÅj√±ƒÅ bhavati sarvvadƒÅ pƒ´·∏∑ƒÅ saiva suh·πõdƒÅmprayacchati mukha≈õobhƒÅ·πÅ kar·πá·πáap≈´ratayƒÅ catura·∏• kalƒÅ
vilƒÅse niyatam ya≈õcƒÅ·πÅdndro bhavatyana·πÅgasya muktƒÅgu·πáastu h·πõdaye muktƒÅgu·πáa Eva
vanitƒÅnƒÅm¬∑ Aga·πáitanarahayakarikulavimarddajanitena re·πáutuhinena ƒÄropita≈õa
≈õima·πá·∏çalasƒÅd·πõ≈õyasahasrakarabimbe pa·π≠aharavagarjjitogre viko≈õanistri·πÅ≈õat¬∑vi
dyudƒÅbhoge pracaritaku√±jarajalade vikƒÅlavar·π£ƒÅvatƒÅra Iva tu·πÅgatura·πÅgatara·πÅge praca
ratkarimakarajanitavi·π£amƒÅvattorte Avira·∏∑amudƒ´r·πá·πáa≈õa·πÅkhe vij·πõmbhamƒÅ·πáe samudra Iva kha·∏çgala
tƒÅvara·πáayute sa≈õarƒÅsananƒÅgatilakapunnƒÅgaghane Uddhatakalakala≈õabde kƒÅnana Iva ca·πá·∏çave
gapavanƒÅkulite yodhƒÅpu≈´rƒ´itadhanu·∏•·π£u vyatipatitapatatriruddhapavanaphpathe pracaritatomara
≈õaktiprƒÅsagadƒÅka·πáayakapprpa·πáacakre Anyonyalƒ´i≈õaradanakulƒ´i≈õasthirakiƒ´litavadanama
ttagajab·πõnde Anyonyam≈´rddhapƒÅtitakha·πÖgavyati·π£aktaturagasƒÅdiga·πáe ≈õastrƒÅ≈õastraikacƒÅ
kacida·πá·∏çƒ´rk·πõiƒÅda·πá·∏çikriyƒÅpravya·πÅktabha·π≠ajane Anyonyasad·πõ≈õaga·πáanaparibhavanƒ´ryyiryƒÅtanƒÅm·πõgabh·πõ≈õamadami≈õrƒ´ita
≈õo·πÅ·πáitaku·πÅkumasghanalipyamƒÅnabh≈´mitale virahitanipatitavƒÅhugrƒ´vƒÅja·πÅghorukƒÅ·πá·∏ça
dantabalauyghe bhvy≈´hasampƒÅtavidƒ´r·πá·πáaprajavitavidrutabh≈´mipatitobhayapak·π£e Anyonyajaya
parƒÅjayasandehapre·πÅkhalagnalak·π£mƒ´vihite rudhiroaighapƒÅlikƒÅyƒ´itapatitagaja≈õre·πái
p·πõ·π£·π≠havicarat¬∑mubha·π≠e AnyonyaghƒÅtarandhrƒÅnadhigamalauptak·πõiriyƒÅyaitasthitayodhe ≈õastro
dyatabhujada·πá·∏çai·∏• sƒÅa·πÅrambhavilohitƒÅk·π£ada·π£·π≠o·π£·π≠hapu·π≠ai rƒÅjanye·∏• k·πõtak·πõtyai·∏• nƒ´ihati
tƒÅrddhahatairitasthitata·∏• sa·πÅkƒ´r·πá·πáe ca ≈õƒ´r·πá·πáadhvajƒÅtapatrai·∏• patitagajaai≈õca sitacalita
cƒÅmaranikare kha·πá·∏çitavim·πõditac≈´r·πá·πáitamaku·π≠a·πÅƒÅ·πÖgadahƒÅraka·π≠akakar·πá·πáƒÅbhara·πáe rudhiramadhupƒÅna
mattapragƒ´tak≈´·π£mƒÅ·πá·∏çarƒÅk·π£asapi≈õƒÅce dattalayatulyakƒÅlapratibhayanƒ´n·πõrtyamkavandha≈õatra
yonau yonekalƒÅak·π£asƒÅdhanamƒÅyodhana≈õirasi vikramƒÅditya·πÅ kaprpa·π≠amƒÅtrapari
cchadam¬∑ EkƒÅkipalƒÅyitam¬∑ k·πõta rannaprabhƒÅkhacitakƒÅ√±cana≈õƒÅribandhasƒÅnnƒÅhya nƒÅgamakivƒÅ
ra·πáanƒÅmadheyam¬∑ . nityƒÅnubandhamadanijaramadrinƒÅtha sƒÅk·π£ƒÅdiva dvipasahasrak·πõtƒÅniuyƒÅtrnam¬∑ . 5
trida≈õapatitura·πÅgasyevama·π£·π≠ama·πÅgalayatre varasa√±calasam¬∑pravyaktakalyƒÅ·πáajƒÅti·πÅ . turagama
ti≈õayƒÅkhyƒÅa·πÅ rannbapalyƒÅ·πáavantam¬∑ sa tamapi hayalak·π£ai≈õcƒÅmaracchannakar·πá·πáai·∏• . 6 samarapari
≈õramasya sad·πõtva≈õamahapalamalayujavokam¬∑ rattanakharamanupamamƒÅ·πáeiyamarakatani
ve≈õama·πá·∏çanam¬∑ ≈õlak·π£·πáagu·πáa·πÅ gu·πáantaka·π≠is≈´tram¬∑ Udƒ´r·πá·πáam¬∑ ma·πáiprabham¬∑ bhƒÅsurakira·πáamƒÅli
ko·π≠amƒÅ·πáikkamanaghamavi≈õ·πõtam¬∑ manasi bhayavirppayanpƒÅrtthivƒÅnƒÅndi≈õi di≈õi ca·π≠itanirtyo
ya≈õam¬∑ pu·π£pamƒÅlƒÅ Idam¬∑ maharada≈õe·π£asaktayƒÅ ≈õaktalak·π£myƒÅ saha vapu·π£ƒ´ vi≈õe·π£ƒÅla·πÅk·πõ
te vƒ´rak·πõtyƒÅ tena parame≈õvare·πáa ≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukko·π≠·π≠amaddhye nƒ´rve·∏∑urnƒÅmamanyavƒÅntararƒÅ·π£·π≠re ma·π£·π≠a≈õa
taccaturvvedakulasam·πõiddhak≈´ragrƒÅmanƒÅmanƒÅmagrƒÅmamaddhye vidyƒÅvinƒ´tapallavaparame≈õvarag·πõhe prati·π£·π≠hƒÅpi
tasya bhagavata·∏• parame·π£·π≠hina·∏• pinƒÅkapƒÅ·πáe·∏• p≈´jyƒÅstrƒÅpanakumumagandhadh≈´padƒ´pahavirupa
hƒÅrabali≈õa·πÅkhapa·π≠ƒÅahƒÅdipravarttanƒÅrttham Atraiva UdakamaƒÅgnibhƒÅratamƒÅƒÅkhyƒÅnanimittƒÅrttha√±ca
ma·πáayikko·π≠·π≠amaddhye patmƒÅnƒÅmamanyavƒÅntararƒÅ·π£·π≠re parame≈õvarama·πÅgalanƒÅmagrƒÅma·∏• devatdƒÅna
nimitta·πÅ brahmadeya·πÅ vidyƒÅvinƒ´tapalla·πÅvƒÅdhirƒÅjaviIyj√±apta·∏•yƒÅ ssarvvadattaparihƒÅre datta·∏•ssarvvada
ttaparihƒÅre datta Iti . AtrƒÅ√±j√±apti·∏• UttarakƒÅra·πáikƒÅmahƒÅsenadatta·∏• vidyƒÅvinƒ´
tapallavaparame≈õvarag·πõha Iha ca devakarmmanavakarmmarthe k≈´rattƒÅcƒÅryyaputraAnanta
≈õivaƒÄcƒÅryya·∏• datta Iti phulla≈õarmmaƒÅ dvau putrapautrƒÅ ƒÄ√±jj√±aptƒÅ·∏• ..

≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukko
·π≠·π≠attu nƒ´rve·∏∑urnƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukk≈´ramum √±amma·πâampƒÅkkamu √±a·πÖka·πáata ·π≠a viccƒÅvinƒ´
tappallavaraca·πâ vileaikkƒÅ·πáa·πÖkeaiyik·πü ko·π≠uttucci ·πáe ceyitu ƒÄyirattirun≈´
·πü·πüukku·∏ªippa·π≠iyƒÅl vi·πü·πüukko·πá·π≠a nilam . ta·∏∑i E·π≠uppata·πüku O·π≠u cu·π≠akko·πá·π≠a ni
lam . taleaippƒÅ·π≠akattu·∏∑ c≈´·∏∑eaime·π≠·π≠uppa·π≠·π≠iyum ≈™ru·∏∑ ma·πá·π≠akam E·π≠utta nila
tto·π≠u·πÖk≈´·π≠a AyintekƒÅl pa·π≠·π≠i nilamum vi·πü·πüu ko·πá·π≠u viccƒÅvinƒ´tapallavaparameccu
rakaram E·π≠uttu Eri to·πá·π≠i Itta·∏∑i va·∏ªipƒÅ·π≠u ceyivƒÅrkku Irukkum mane·πâaiyum mane·πâaippa
·π≠appum vakuttu Itanu·∏∑ mikka nilam O·∏ªukkavikku vi·∏∑eai nilamƒÅkavum . I·πânilattukku
kƒ´·∏ªpƒÅlellai m≈´utukƒÅ·π≠·π≠u va·∏ªiyi·πâ me·πükum . te·πâpƒÅlellai ≈™r puku va·∏ªiyi·πâ va·π≠akku
m . melpƒÅlellai ≈™r puku va·∏ªi ni·πâ·πüum va·π≠akku nokki nƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukkƒÅlukke po·πâa va·∏ªi
yi·πâ ki·∏ªakkum . va·π≠apƒÅlellai nƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukkƒÅlin te·πükum . I·πânƒÅ·πâkelleai Akattum
ta·∏∑iyum Eriyum va·∏ªipƒÅ·π≠u ceyivƒÅrkku manaiyum manaippa·π≠appu·πânƒ´kki mik
ka nilamum c≈´·∏∑aime·π≠·π≠uppa·π≠·π≠iyum O·∏ªukkavikku vi·∏∑ai nilamƒÅka ko·π≠uttu It
ta·∏∑ikku ve·πá·π≠untevakarumanavakaruma√±ceyivata·πükum Irupati·πâmar caturppetika
·∏∑ukkuppiramateya·πÖko·π≠uppata·πükum ma·πáayi·πüko·π≠·π≠attuppa·πâmƒÅnƒÅ·π≠·π≠uppara
meccurama·πÖkalattu·∏∑ Akappa·π≠·π≠a va·∏∑eaiyattil cu·πü·πüu nilam Attanaiyum Irupat
teyintu pa·πÖkƒÅyi . Ita·πâu·∏∑ m≈´·πâ·πüu pa·πÖkum k≈´rattutta·∏∑ikku tevakarumanavakaruma√±cey
vatƒÅkavum . k≈´rattu ma·πá·π≠akattukku ta·πá·πáƒ´rkkuntƒ´kkum Oru pa·πÖkƒÅkavum . Imma·πá·π≠akatte pƒÅ
ratam vƒÅcippata·πüku Oru pa·πÖkƒÅkavum . ni·πâ·πüa Irupatu pa·πÖkum Irupati·πâmar caturppetika
·∏∑ukku pimramateyamƒÅka ko·π≠ututtatu . Ivv≈´r manaiyum manaippa·π≠appum ≈™rƒÅ·∏∑cciyum cek
kum ta·πüiyum k≈´lamum tarakum kattikkƒÅ·πáamum ma·πü·πüum potuvinƒÅllu·∏∑·∏∑atellƒÅm Ip
pa·πÖku Irupatte·πâaiti·πâ va·∏ªiyƒÅle pa·πÖki·π≠·π≠u·πápƒÅrrƒÅkavum . Ivv≈´r parameccurata·π≠ƒÅkat
tukku pƒÅlƒÅ·πü·πüu ni·πâ·πüum to·πá·π≠i·πâa perumpi·π≠uku kƒÅlin pu·∏ªuti pƒÅ·π≠um nƒ´rrƒ´·πâta va·∏ªiyƒÅle
talevƒÅyum taleppe·∏ªaiyum ≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅlum to·πá·π≠i·πâa nilamellƒÅm paramecu
varama·πÖkalattu nilamƒÅka IkkƒÅlu·∏∑ ku·πü·πüetta
maiyum nƒÅ·π≠ƒÅ·∏ªcciyu·πânƒÅ·π≠·π≠uppa·πá·πáikkeyum
tƒÅ·πâa·πÖko·π≠utta mu·πâ·πüu pa·πÖkunu·∏∑um O·πâ·πüaraippa·πÖku
nƒÅr A·πâantacivaƒÅcƒÅriyar makka·∏∑ makka·∏∑ marumakka·∏∑
ppu·πüamkko·πá·π≠u va·∏ªipƒÅ·π≠u ceyitu palipoca·πâa
m ko·πá·π≠u pullacarumar makka·∏∑ makka·∏∑
vakarumamum Ivviruvar makka·∏∑ makka·∏∑
devatamƒÅkƒÅni·πâ·πüa bappabha·π≠·π≠ƒÅrakaru·πÖkƒÅ
kkƒÅttu ko·π≠ukka ..

AsyƒÅ·∏• pra≈õasteravatƒÅ ‚èë - - - - ‚èë - - ‚èë ‚èë - ‚èë - - .
soma≈õca yasyƒÅssahavƒÅsabandhustraya ‚èë - - ‚èë ‚èë - ‚èë - - . x x x x
hmadatta√±ca dvidhƒÅ bhakti√±ca pƒÅtu ya·∏• . macchiramma x x x x x x x x ‚èë - ‚èë - . x x x x x
pu·π£·π≠ƒÅni vƒÅhanƒÅni mahƒ´pate . yuddhakƒÅle x x x x x x x x ‚èë - ‚èë - .
devasvambraƒÅhma·πáasva√±ca lobhenopahinasti ya·∏• . x x x x x x x x x x
cchi·π£·π≠ena jƒ´vani .. bh≈´midƒÅnƒÅtparandƒÅnam na bh≈´toa·πÅ na bhavi·π£yati . x x x x x x x x x
bh≈´to na bhavi·π£yati . la .

app.note: tadudaya¬∞ seems to be corrected from tadanvaya¬∫.
The r≈õa of ¬∫dar≈õana·∏• seems to be corrected from ≈õi.
The gha of ghana seems to be corrected from sa.
The whole passage from mpƒÅta to A is engraved over an erasure and partially doubtful.
nna is a correction for some other group; read ranna¬∫.
The passage from ¬∫ma·π£·π≠a¬∫ to ¬∫sam¬∑ is corrupt and against the metre.
to·πá is engraved over an erasure.
There follows an illegible scrawl, which fills the remainder of line 95, and two further lines.

div.head: A. Sanskrit portion.
div.p: Hail!Owing to the bad preservation of the first plate, both the reading and the translation of verses 1 and 2 are merely tentative. (Verse 1.) May (≈öiva) protect us, who has five faces (and) fifteen fearful eyes, who bears the moon on his crest, who wears the trident, whose sacred thread is a terrible serpent, who possesses ten strong arms, who has the form of the universal soul which consists of truth alone, the divine one, who is to be respected by Mukunda (Vish·πáu) and the other immortals, who produces the creation, who is propitiated by spells, the creator, (who is) knowledge incarnate, who performs perfect self-restraint, and whose form is the universe!

(Verse 2.) Victorious is that Parame≈õvara (≈öiva), who consists of the three Vedas, the crest-jewel of the three worlds, who places in the hearts of beings the power which effects actions, the moon of the highest sky, the succession of whose particles (causes) a multitude of products, and whose rays crystallize, when they fall, as on a moon-stone, on the mind of the learned!

(Verse 3.) May that race of the Pallavas,‚Äîin which we hear no prince was (ever) born, who was not pious, who did not perform the soma sacrifice, who raised the club of war unjustly, who was a sham saint, who did not perform heroic deeds (only for the sake of) liberality, whose tongue was so false as to speak an untruth, or who was alarmed in battles, ‚Äîbe unobstructed in protecting the earth, which is free from calamities!

(Line 9.) From Brahman (sprang) A·πÖgiras; from him, B·πõihaspati; from him, BharadvƒÅja; from him, Dro·πáa; from Dro·πáa, A≈õvatthƒÅman, the splendour of whose power was immeasurable; from him, Pallava, who drove away (every) jot of a calamity from his race; from him, the race of the Pallavas, the favourites of the whole world.

(Verse 4.) May that Pallava race last (for ever), in which we have heard no prince was (ever) born, who was not pious, who was not liberal, (or) who was not brave!The same sentiment is expressed in different words in verse 3.

(Line 12.) The grandson of Narasi·πÅhavarman, (who arose) from the kings of this race, just as the moon and the sun from the eastern mountain; who was the crest-jewel on the head of those princes, who had never bowed their heads (before); who proved a lion to the elephant-herd of hostile kings; who appeared to be the blessed Narasi·πÅha himself, who had come down (to earth) in the shape of a prince; who repeatedly defeated the Cho·∏∑as, Kera·∏∑as, Ka·∏∑abhras, and PƒÅ·πá·∏çyas; who, like SahasrabƒÅhu (i.e., the thousand-armed KƒÅrtavƒ´rya), enjoyed the action of a thousand arms in hundreds of fights; who wrote the (three) syllables of (the word) vijaya (i.e., victory), as on a plate, on Pulake≈õin's back, which was caused to be visible (i.e., whom he caused to turn his back) in the battles of Pariya·∏∑a, Ma·πáima·πÖgala, ≈ö≈´ramƒÅra, etc.; and who destroyed (the city of) VƒÅtƒÅpi, just as the pitcher-born (Agastya) (the demon) VƒÅtƒÅpi;‚Äî

(Line 17.) The son of Mahendravarman, by whom prosperity was thoroughly produced (su-rachita), just as prosperity is heaped on the gods (sura-chita) by Mahendra; and who thoroughly enforced the sacred law of the castes and the orders;‚Äî

(Line 19.) (was) Parame≈õvaravarman, whose beauty (dar≈õana) surpassed (that of) all (others), just as Parame≈õvara (≈öiva) has (one) eye (dar≈õana) more than all (others); who, like Bharata, was a conqueror of all; who avoided improper conduct (asama√±jasa), just as Sagara abandoned (his son) Asama√±jasaElsewhere called Asama√±ja or Asama√±jas.; who possessed a strong body (a·πÖga), just as Kar·πáa was (king) of the prosperous A·πÖgas; who was fond of poems (kƒÅvya), just as YayƒÅti of (his father-in-law) KƒÅvya (U≈õanas); whose command always caused pain to haughty kings, like a chaplet (forcibly placed on their heads),The following play on kar·πáap≈´ra, ‚Äúfilling the ears‚Äù and ‚Äúan ear-ring,‚Äù suggests that the composer intended to make a similar pun on pi·∏çƒÅ, ‚Äúpain,‚Äù and ƒÅpi·∏çƒÅ, ‚Äúa chaplet.‚Äù ƒÄpƒ´·∏ça is elsewhere only used as a masculine. but gave splendour to the faces of friends by reaching their ears, like an ear-ring; who was constantly clever in the sport of the fine arts (kalƒÅ), (just as) the moon is charming in the beauty of her digits (kalƒÅ); (who resembled) the string of pearls (muktƒÅgu·πáa) on the breast of Cupid, but who, at the same time, avoided unlawful (intercourse) with women (even) by thought.The second muktƒÅgu·πáa stands for mukta·∏• agu·πáa·∏• yena, and agu·πáa for adharma. With the irregular construction vanitƒÅnƒÅ·πÅ muktƒÅgu·πáa·∏• for muktavanitƒÅgu·πáa·∏•, compare kshataraksha·πáa·πÅ diva·∏• for kshatadyuraksha·πáam in the ≈öi≈õupƒÅlavadha, sarga i, verse 48.

(Line 23.) At the head of a battle,‚Äîin which the disk of the sun was caused to assume the likeness of the circle of the moon through the mist of the dust, that was produced by the marching of countless troops of men, horses and elephants, which was terrible through the thunder-like sound of drums, which teemed with unsheathed swords that resembled flashes of lightning, in which elephants were moving like clouds, and which (therefore) resembled an unseasonable appearance of the rainy season; in which tall horses looked like billows, in which elephants caused distress on their path, just as sea-monsters produce whirlpools, in which conches were incessantly blown (or cast up), and which (therefore) resembled the gaping ocean; which was full of swords and shields (ƒÅvara·πáa), just as of rhinoceroses, creepers and vara·πáa (trees), which was crowded with heroes who possessed bows and mighty elephants, as if it were crowded with ≈õara (grass) and with asana, nƒÅga, tilaka and pu·πÅnƒÅga (trees), in which confused noises were raised, and which (therefore) appeared to be a forest; which was agitated by a violent wind, (but) in which the path of the wind was obstructed by arrows, that flew past each other on the bows (themselves), while these were bent by the warriors; in which javelins, pikes, darts, clubs, lances, spears and discuses were flying about; in which troops of furious elephants firmly impaled each other's faces with the piercing thunderbolts of their tusks; in which squadrons of horsemen were connected by their swords, that had struck each other's heads; in which there were soldiers who were noted (for their dexterity) in fighting with sword against sword, (pulling of) hair against (pulling of) hair, and club against club; in which the ground was thickly smeared with saffron, as the blood was mixed with the copious rutting-juice of elephants, that issued in consequence of (their) considering each other as equals (or) despising each other; in which (both) large armies had lost and dropped arms, necks, shanks, thighbones and teeth; in which, owing to the encounter of the armies, both sides were broken, urged on, put to flight and prostrated on the ground; which was attended by the goddess of fortune, sitting on the swing of the doubt about mutual victory or defeat; in which brave warriors were marching on the back of lines of fallen elephants, that formed a bridge over the flood of blood; in which soldiers stood motionless,With the denominative luptakriyƒÅyita compare pƒÅlikƒÅyita in line 34. if their blows did not hit each other's weak parts; which was covered here and there with shattered banners and parasols, with fallen elephants and with dead and half-dead soldiers, who had done their duty, whose strong arms (still) raised the weapon, whose lips were bitten and whose eyes were deep-red with fury; in which a multitude of white chƒÅmaras was waving; in which tiaras, armlets, necklaces, bracelets and ear-rings were broken, crushed and pulverized; in which the K≈´shmƒÅ·πá·∏ças, RƒÅkshasas and Pi≈õƒÅchas were singing, intoxicated with drinking the liquor of blood; and which contained hundreds of headless trunks, that were vehemently dancing together in a fearful manner according to the beaten time,‚Äîhe, unaided, made VikramƒÅditya, whose army consisted of several lakshas, take to flight, covered only by a rag.

(Verses 5 and 6.) He, having caused to be accoutred the elephant called ArivƒÅra·πáa (i.e., ‚Äòwarding off enemies‚Äô), whose golden saddle was covered with the splendour of jewels, whose rut was perpetual, who (therefore) appeared to be the king of mountains himself whose torrents never cease to flow, and who was followed by thousands of (other) elephants,‚Äî and the horse called Ati≈õaya (i.e., ‚Äòeminence‚Äô), whose noble breed was manifest, and who wore a saddle (set with) jewels, together with lakshas of (other) horses, whose ears were covered with chƒÅmaras . . . . . . . . . . . .Part of verse 6 and the whole of the ensuing prose passage (lines 44 to 49) are corrupt and therefore left untranslated.

(Line 49.) This Parame≈õvara gave to the blessed lord PinƒÅkapƒÅ·πái (≈öiva),‚Äîwho had been placed in the temple of VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta-Pallava-Parame≈õvara in the midst of the village called K≈´ra, which possessed one hundred and eight families that studied the four Vedas, (and which was situated) in the manyavƒÅntara-rƒÅsh·π≠ra called Nƒ´rve·∏∑≈´r, in the midst of ≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukko·π≠·π≠a, in order to provide for the worship, the bathing (of the idol), flowers, perfumes, incense, lamps, oblations (havir-upahƒÅra-bali), conches, drums, etc., and for water, fire and the recitation of the BhƒÅrata at this (temple),‚Äîthe village called Parame≈õvarama·πÖgala in the manyavƒÅntara-rƒÅsh·π≠ra called PatmƒÅ, in the midst of Ma·πáayi[·πü]ko·π≠·π≠a, as a divine gift (and) as a gift to BrƒÅhma·πáas, at the request of VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta, the lord of the Pallavas, with exemption from all taxes. The executor (ƒÅj√±aptiSee page 33, note 1.) of this (grant was) MahƒÅsenadatta (of) UttarakƒÅra·πáikƒÅ. And for (performing) the divine rites and the repairs of this temple of VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta-Pallava-Parame≈õvara,‚ÄîAnanta≈õiva-ƒÅchƒÅrya, the son of K≈´ratt-ƒÅchƒÅrya, was given (!), and secondly Phulla≈õarman; (their) sons and grandsons were (also) appointed.

div.head: B. Tamil portion.
div.p: (Line 57.) (At) K≈´ram and √ëamma·πâambƒÅkkam . . . . . . . . . . in Nƒ´rve·∏∑≈´rnƒÅ·∏çu, (a division) of ≈™·πü·πüukkƒÅ·π≠·π≠ukko·π≠·π≠am,‚ÄîVidyƒÅvinƒ´ta, the Pallava king, bought one thousand and two hundred ku·πõisSee page 92, note 4. of land, for which he paid the price in gold. (Other) land was purchased, in order to burn tiles for building a temple. After the pa·π≠·π≠iSee page 91, note 6. of ≈ö≈´·∏∑aime·∏çu within TalaippƒÅ·∏çagam and five and a quarter pa·π≠·π≠is of land in the village, together with the land on which the ma·πá·∏çapa was built, were bought; after the temple of VidyƒÅvinƒ´ta-Pallava-Parame≈õvara was built; after the tank was dug; and after houses and house-gardens were allotted to those, who had to perform the worship at this temple,‚Äîthe land, which remained, was to be cultivated for (providing) the customary offerings. The eastern boundary of this land is to the west of the road to the burningground; the southern boundary is to the north of the road, which leads into the village; the western boundary is to the east of the road, which leads to the district-channel (?) (and which is) on the north of the road, which leads into the village; the northern boundary is to the south of the district-channel. After the land included within these four boundaries,‚Äî with the exception of the temple, the tank, and the houses and house-gardens for those, who had to perform the worship,‚Äîand the pa·π≠·π≠i of ≈ö≈´·∏∑aime·∏çu had been given as land to be cultivated for (providing) the customary offerings,‚Äîthe whole land round the tank (?) in (the village of) Parame≈õvarama·πÖgalam in Pa·πâmƒÅ-nƒÅ·∏çu, (a division) of Ma·πáayi·πüko·π≠·π≠am, (was divided) into twenty-five parts (and set aside) for performing the divine rites and the repairs necessary for this temple, and in order to grant a brahmadeya to twenty Chaturvedins. Of these, three parts shall be (for) performing the divine rites and the repairs of the temple at K≈´ram; one part shall be for water and fire for the ma·πá·∏çapa at K≈´ram; one part shall be for reciting the BhƒÅrata in this ma·πá·∏çapa; the remaining twenty parts were given as a brahmadeya to twenty Chaturvedins. (The donees) shall enjoy the houses and house-gardens of this village, the village-property (?), the oil-mills, the looms, the bƒÅzƒÅr, the brokerage, the kattikkƒÅ·πáam (?) and all other common (property), after (the proceeds) have been divided in the proportion of these twenty-five parts. The dry land (?) (along) the Perumbi·∏çugu channel, which was dug from the PƒÅlƒÅ·πüuThis is the PƒÅlƒÅr river, which flows to the south of Little KƒÅ√±chƒ´. It is also mentioned in No. 59, line 5. to the tank of Parame≈õvara at this village, (and) all the land, in which . . . . . . . . . . channels (from) fountains were dug, (shall be) the land of Parame≈õvarama·πÖgalam . . . . . . . . . .
(Line 83.) Of the three parts, which were given, Ananta≈õiva-ƒÅchƒÅrya and his sons and further descendants (shall enjoy) one and a half part . . . . . . . . . .
(Line 86.) Phulla≈õarman and his sons and further descendants . . . . . . . . . .

div.head: [[C.]]
div.p: [Lines 89 to 95 contain fragments of five Sanskrit verses, in the first of which the inscription is called a pra≈õastiAccording to Mr. Fleet, Corpus Inscriptionum Indicarum, Vol. III, p. 87, note 10, the only other instance, in which the term pra≈õasti is applied to an inscription on copper-plates, is the Chicacole grant of the mahƒÅrƒÅja Indravarman, Ind. Ant. Vol. XIII, p. 121. or eulogy; the remaining four were, as usual, imprecatory verses.]
Digital edition of SII 1.151 by converted to DHARMA conventions by Emmanuel Francis.
See revised edition by Emmanuel Francis (Pallava 46).
